tutorial.sdeflist: The full index of sound events is located in your DCS folder at \\DCS World\\Doc\\Sounds\\sdef_and_wave_list.txt. Open the SDEF List tab to learn more.
tutorial.library: This is where you browse and select DCS sound assets. Navigate the folder tree and check the files you want to replace with your custom audio.
tutorial.project: Here you configure your mod details — name, aircraft, version. All selected assets appear here so you can review them.
tutorial.sdef: Each sound asset needs an .sdef file. The visual editor lets you tweak volume, looping, distance, pitch and more — no code needed!
tutorial.theme: Customise the look of the app. Change the background image and accent colors to make it feel like yours.
tutorial.presets: Save and load your SDEF parameter presets. Great for reusing settings across different mods.
tutorial.collab: Connect to a GitHub repo to sync your project with teammates. Push your mod build, leave notes, and restore earlier versions.
tutorial.build: When you're ready, hit Build to generate the complete mod folder — entry.lua, .sdef files, and your audio — ready to drop into DCS Mods.
tutorial.docs: Everything you need to know about DCS sound modding: file formats, parameters, tips and examples.
tutorial.credits: Info about the app, the developer, and useful community links. You're all set — happy modding! 🎧

defaults.title: Recommended Defaults per Sound Type
defaults.desc: The mod creator auto-fills these values based on sound type:
defaults.note: Note: A "—" means the default of the engine is used.

audio.title: 🎵 Audio Guide for DCS Sound Modding

monoStereo.title: Mono vs Stereo
monoStereo.tip: Rule of thumb: If DCS needs to position the sound in 3D space (engines, switches, weapons), use Mono. Stereo is primarily for ambient/atmospheric sounds and UI effects.

loopOneshot.title: Loop vs One-Shot
loopOneshot.desc: DCS distinguishes between two playback modes:
loopOneshot.loop: Loop: Sound plays continuously until stopped (engines, APU, wind, avionics hum). These need seamless loop points.
loopOneshot.oneshot: One-shot: Sound plays once and stops (button clicks, switch toggles, explosions, weapon launches). Set detached = true in the SDEF.
loopOneshot.warning: Important: Looping sounds should have clean loop points — avoid clicks or pops at the start/end boundaries. Use a DAW to crossfade the loop points.

whatIs.title: What is a .sdef file?
whatIs.p1: A .sdef (Source DEFinition) file tells DCS how to play a sound. It defines which audio file to use, its volume, spatial properties, playback mode, and more.
whatIs.p2: SDEF files live in Sounds/sdef/ and reference audio files in Sounds/Effects/.

theme.title: 🎨 Theme Customization Guide

whatIs.title: What is a DCS Theme?
whatIs.desc: A theme allows you to customize the look of DCS World's user interface — including the main menu background, loading screen, briefing map, and mod icon.

fileRequirements.title: File Requirements
fileRequirements.iconUsage: Mod icon in the DCS module manager

fileRequirements.loadingUsage: Background image shown during mission loading
fileRequirements.briefingUsage: Background of the mission briefing map screen

formatRecommendations.title: Format Recommendations
formatRecommendations.resolution: Use the exact recommended resolutions for best results
formatRecommendations.hd: Loading and menu backgrounds should be 1920×1080 for full HD displays
formatRecommendations.vram: Larger images may work but will use more VRAM

theme.tip: Theme support must be enabled in the entry.lua with a Skins section and mount_vfs_texture_path. The Build page handles this automatically when you enable Theme.

install.title: 📦 How to Install a DCS Sound Mod
install.step1: Navigate to your DCS saved games folder:
install.step2: Copy your entire mod folder (e.g., SoundModBetterHornet/) into the resource folder.
install.step4: Your mod should be automatically detected!

gain.title: Gain-Distance Model
gain.desc: DCS uses a multi-stage gain curve based on distance. This visual model shows how volume (gain) changes as you move away.

rampUp.title: 1. Ramp Up
rampUp.desc: From silent_radius to peak_radius. Sound fades in linearly from 0 to 100%.

peak.title: 2. Peak Zone
peak.desc: At peak_radius, the volume is at its maximum (Gain = 1.0).

reference.title: 3. Reference
reference.desc: At inner_radius, volume is defined as 0.85 of max. This is the standard DCS reference point.

falloff.title: 4. Falloff
falloff.desc: From inner_radius to outer_radius, volume drops following an inverse curve to 0.

f18.title: F/A-18C Sound Reference
f18.desc: Detailed guide for Hornet sound modding. Reference these paths and settings for your custom sounds.
f18.tipMono: Engines & Cockpit: Most exterior sounds and interior switches are Mono for 3D positioning.
f18.warningLoop: Engine Loops: Ensure your startup/shutdown/idle loops have precise crossfades to avoid popping.

tips.enginePitch: Engines use pitch modulation based on RPM range.
tips.cockpitQuiet: Keep cockpit switches gain around 0.6-0.8 for realism.
tips.explosionsDist: Explosions need large outer_radius (15000+) to be heard from distance.
tips.randomListDesc: Use a list of files for wave to avoid repetitive sound patterns.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.desc: Learn how to use Better ModMaker and create DCS sound mods.
docs.loadSoundsDesc: When DCS needs a sound, it looks for the SDEF file at the specified path. The SDEF tells DCS:

loadSoundsSteps.2: At what volume to play it (gain)
loadSoundsSteps.3: Where it comes from in 3D space (position, inner_radius, outer_radius)
loadSoundsSteps.4: How to play it (loop, oneshot, random selection)

docs.overridePriority: Override Priority: When you mount your Sounds/ folder using mount_vfs_sound_path, your SDEF files take priority over DCS defaults. You only need to include files you want to change.

examples.engine: Example: Engine Loop with Directional Cone

audio.title: Audio Guide for DCS Sound Modding

f18.title: F/A-18C Complete Sound Reference
f18.desc: Based on the BetterHornet mod — every SDEF file and WAV detailed.

sdeflist.title: Where to find sdef_and_wave_list.txt
sdeflist.desc: The file sdef_and_wave_list.txt contains the full exhaustive list of all sound events and parameters inside DCS World.
sdeflist.tip: You can find it in the installation directory of DCS World. Usually located in your Program Files or Saved Games depending on your setup.

audio.title: Audio Format
audio.tip: DCS supports both .wav and .ogg formats. WAV is higher quality, OGG is smaller.
audio.warning: Browser OGG encoding is limited — WAV conversion is recommended.

action.noAssets: No assets selected — go to the Library to select sounds

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.description: Find answers to common questions about using Better ModMaker.

q1.title: How to update Assets Library?
q1.desc: Go to the Docs tab, click 'Reload Library', and make sure your DCS directory is correctly mapped. The application will scan and update your internal reference.

q2.title: Where is sdef_and_wave_list.txt located?
q2.desc: It is generated by DCS and can be found in your DCS installation folder under \\Doc\\Sounds\\sdef_and_wave_list.txt. Better ModMaker automatically references it if correctly set up.

q3.title: Can you make a mod for another mod?
q3.desc: Yes! The Asset Library recursively loads modded aircraft paths alongside default DCS modules. If your custom mod adds its own sound references, you can find them and override them the normal way.

q4.title: How do I install a generated mod?
q4.desc: Build your mod from the Build tab using OVGME format or a direct folder. Drag the folder into your Saved Games\\DCS\\Mods or use a Mod Manager like OVGME/OpenModManager. For complex instructions, visit

q5.title: How can I participate/contribute or report a bug?
q5.desc: You can contribute via GitHub (Fork the repository and propose Pull Requests) or join the Discord for live feedback and bug reports. Contributions and bug reports are highly appreciated!

q6.title: Are ALL aircraft present in the Library?
q6.desc: Yes, ALL default core aircraft and unencrypted mods are present in the Assets Library. However, finding specific sounds may be long and complex. We recommend using Community Presets to automatically filter sounds for specific actions.

q7.title: How to import modded aircraft SDEFs into the Assets Library?
q7.desc: To import modded aircraft sounds, locate the mod's folder and find the 'Sounds' directory. For mods in 'Saved Games', it's usually at the root (e.g., A4E/Sounds). For 'CoreMods' (DCS root), search in CoreMods\aircraft\{AIRCRAFTNAME}\Sounds. You can either zip the Sounds folder as a .zip file or simply zip the entire mod and use the Import Mod (.zip) option in the Library tab.
